Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I work part-time and money is tight, so I had to fit studying around my shifts. I'd usually block out chunks of time on my days off—maybe 4 or 5 hours at a stretch—and just grind through reading materials and notes. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time transcribing lecture content into my own words in notebooks. It wasn't elegant, but it worked for me given everything else I was juggling.
Q2: My pattern varied massively depending on what was due and when I could actually sit down without distractions. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because work picked up or I was just mentally exhausted. Then right before deadlines, I'd have these intense study marathons where I'd put in 8-10 hours over a couple of days. Regularity was a luxury I couldn't really afford.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or wanting to check if there was new content I needed to download. Sometimes I'd log in just to see discussion posts or if anyone had asked questions I could learn from. Honestly, boredom also played a role—when I was stuck at home with nothing to do, I'd scroll through the LMS looking for something to engage with.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine with coffee, just a quick scroll to see what was happening. That habit formed when I realized I was missing announcements and falling behind. But it never felt natural—more like a chore I forced myself to do. When stress got really bad, even that small routine fell apart.
Q5: The reading materials and any written summaries were gold for me because I learn best by reading and rewriting things. Discussion forums were surprisingly useful too—seeing how other students interpreted concepts helped me understand things from different angles. The videos were okay but I found myself taking notes from them and then never rewatching, just using my notes instead.
Q6: I mostly ignored the optional quizzes and some of the supplementary videos because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to complete the basics. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what would actually count toward my grade. Some resources just seemed redundant—like why watch a 30-minute video when I could read the same content in 10 minutes?
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I'd be tested on or if it contributed to my final grade. I also valued anything that made complex concepts click—like a well-written explanation or a discussion thread where someone broke things down simply. If something felt like busywork or padding, I'd skip it without guilt.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. Like, they're there to help, but when you're already drowning in required work and life responsibilities, they just add to the overwhelm. Required activities had a clear purpose and deadline, which actually made them easier to prioritize. Optional stuff made me anxious because I felt like I should do it but couldn't.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them on paper with a pen, making notes in the margins and creating summary sheets. I also used some free textbook resources I found online and YouTube videos for topics I didn't understand. My LMS activity probably showed a fraction of the actual work I put in.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk for hours working through printed materials and handwritten notes, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study on the bus to work using downloaded content on my phone. Group study sessions with classmates happened in person—we'd quiz each other and discuss concepts, but none of that registered anywhere online.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for that module, and then work through it over the next week or two without touching the LMS again. It was more efficient for me and saved data since my internet situation isn't always stable. The LMS probably thought I disappeared, but I was actually deep in study mode.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal—my anxiety was through the roof, work was demanding more hours, and I felt like I was falling behind in everything. There were weeks where just opening my laptop felt overwhelming. The CBT sessions helped me manage the worst of it, but there were definitely dark periods where engaging with anything academic felt impossible.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but just stare at the screen unable to process anything. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid them until the last minute because I didn't know where to start. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often lost out to more immediate survival needs like earning money.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times I'd have the LMS open while my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills, replaying stressful conversations, just zoning out. I'd scroll through content without really absorbing it, or watch a video while thinking about other things. My body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like there was this wall between me and the work. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with the same paralysis. It wasn't laziness; it was genuine mental block that I had to work through in therapy.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have this pattern of low activity for weeks, then frantic engagement right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline cut through the anxiety and boredom in a way that nothing else did. The LMS became a last-minute battlefield.
Q17: When I got decent feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and start questioning whether my effort even mattered. Positive feedback after my exam score was a huge boost—it reminded me I could actually do this despite everything working against me.
Q18: The course structure was a double-edged sword. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but the sheer amount of content felt overwhelming given my limited time. The flexibility of online learning was supposed to help, but it also meant I could keep postponing things. More check-ins or structured milestones might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because they felt more human and interactive. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many small tasks that felt disconnected from the bigger picture. Also, the platform itself was clunky sometimes, which added friction when I was already low on motivation.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—spending time reading discussions, downloading materials, trying to understand content, but struggling to actually complete and submit assignments. High activity might mean genuine engagement with learning even if the output isn't there. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism making it hard to finalize work.
Q21: That's basically my story. My exam score was decent and I got a Merit, but my LMS activity was pretty minimal because I did most of my work offline. Low activity doesn't mean low effort—it might mean someone has found efficient ways to learn outside the system, or they're dealing with circumstances that limit their online presence.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, submission times. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental state behind the clicks. They can't capture someone staring at a screen in a panic or someone deeply focused on printed notes. Numbers without context can be really misleading.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, when I want to figure things out, when I'm connecting new information to what I already know. It's mental investment, not just time spent. Some of my most engaged moments happened far from any computer.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite financial stress, anxiety, and feeling like I didn't belong. It can't show the CBT sessions that helped me manage my mental health enough to keep going. It can't show the pride I felt when I realized I could actually succeed despite everything. My resilience is invisible in the data.
